title: Chronic Ischemic Heart Disease Selection of Treatment Modality -- Introduction
content: The term stable ischemic heart disease (SIHD) often is used synonymously with chronic coronary artery disease (CAD) and encompasses a variety of conditions where the end result is a repetitive mismatch between myocardial oxygen supply and demand. This most frequently is seen when long-standing atherosclerotic obstruction within the epicardial coronary arteries results in poor flow and ischemia distally. However, this is not the only mechanism. Various pathophysiologic processes such as coronary artery vasospasm, microcirculation dysfunction, or congenital anomalies can cause the same supply-demand mismatch and result in chronic repetitive ischemia. [1] [2] [3] [2]

title: Selecting a Treatment Modality in Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Issues of Concern
content: One of the significant issues in the management of patients who had ACS is the optimal strategy to balance ischemic risk and bleeding risk in patients who require long-term anticoagulation due to another specific indication, as these patients already require antiplatelet therapy post-ACS. This type of treatment has the name triple therapy. In the most recent ACC/AHA guideline on UA/NSTEMI, the recommendation is that patients who had ACS and underwent PCI should receive triple therapy, but with making every effort to minimize the duration to limit the risk of bleeding. [55] Literature reports unequivocally increased risk of bleeding in patients who are on triple therapy when compared to DAPT or single antiplatelet agent. [55] [91] [92] Several strategies have been proposed to mitigate those risks. These include the use of clinical calculators such as HAS-BLED to assess bleeding risk, evaluating the appropriateness of indication for anticoagulation, targeting the international normalized ratio (INR) to 2 to 2.5 when using warfarin, the use of clopidogrel instead of more potent P2Y12 inhibitors, and the use of proton pump inhibitors in patients with a prior history or at risk of gastrointestinal bleeding. [90] The most recent guidelines for the management of ACS recommend that triple therapy continues for six months following PCI in patients with lower bleeding risk, assessed by HAS-BLED score is equal to or less than 2, followed by an oral anticoagulant (OAC) and clopidogrel or aspirin for the remaining 6 months. [7] [11] [55] In patients with HAS-BLED score of 3 and above, the recommendation is that triple therapy should be administered for one month, followed by an oral anticoagulant (OAC) and clopidogrel or aspirin for the total duration of 12 months. The choice of OAC involves a vitamin K antagonist (VKA) or a non-VKA novel oral anticoagulant (NOAC), based on the patient's preference and other socioeconomic factors. The decision between aspirin and clopidogrel during the period of treatment with OAC and one antiplatelet agent depends on the balance of ischemic risks such as complex coronary stenting, left main disease, bifurcation lesions, patients with diabetes, and bleeding risks such as a history of major bleeding and predisposition for future bleeding events. After 12 months of the described treatment, the guidelines recommend that OAC should be continued as a monotherapy indefinitely for the original indication. [7] [11] [55] In patients with ACS who did not undergo revascularization or have undergone CABG, the recommendation is that dual therapy involving OAC and aspirin or clopidogrel should continue for 12 months, and after completing that time, OAC should remain in place. Regardless of the type of revascularization, patients with high ischemic risk may continue OAC and aspirin beyond the 12-month time frame. Several recent trial results have disputed the concept of triple therapy and examined the effect of OAC along with P2Y12 inhibitor in patients with ACS who are undergoing PCI. [93] [94] [95] [96] WOEST, PIONEER-AF, RE-DUAL [96] , and AUGUSTUS trials have examined the efficacy and safety of warfarin, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, and apixaban, respectively, in conjunction with clopidogrel for treatment of patients with ACS who underwent PCI, compared to treatment with triple therapy. [93] [95] [96] [97] Based on the reported results, it appears that dual therapy with an anticoagulant agent and P2Y12 inhibitor, when compared to triple therapy, significantly decreases the bleeding risk, while ischemic and thromboembolic risk remains unchanged. Based on these studies, new practice-changing guidelines are warranted, and the data requires interpretation for safe use in clinical practice. The ongoing ENTRUST-PCI study (NCT02866175) will provide data on edoxaban in this type of clinical scenario. It is worth noting that patients receiving anticoagulation for mechanical valve should exclusively be on warfarin, while the antiplatelet regimen strategy is essentially the same as described above.

title: Selecting a Treatment Modality in Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Function
content: Fibrinolysis should be a consideration if the patient first presents to a non-PCI capable facility and the anticipated time from first medical contact to the performance of primary PCI exceeds 120 minutes (this 120-minute duration usually accounts for a 30-minute door in-door out time (DIDO) time goal at the non-PCI capable facility). Upon deciding about whether or not to use fibrinolysis, it should preferably occur within the first 30 minutes of the first medical contact. This strategy can be achieved either as prehospital fibrinolysis by the emergency medical service (EMS) provider or within 30 minutes of arriving at a non-PCI capable facility (door to needle time). Contraindications to fibrinolysis require prompt review if this strategy is an option. Absolute contraindications are any previous intracranial hemorrhage, ischemic stroke in the past 3 months, central nervous system neoplasm or arteriovenous malformation, major head trauma or surgery within the past month, known active bleeding or bleeding diathesis (with the exception of menses), suspected aortic dissection, severe uncontrolled hypertension unresponsive to emergent medical therapy and use of streptokinase in the last 6 months. [7] Some relative contraindications include a history of chronic poorly controlled hypertension, surgery in the last 3 months, ischemic stroke more than 3 months ago, traumatic or prolonged cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR), and any other unspecified intracranial pathology. Some factors merit consideration when considering fibrinolysis based on the available evidence from the recent clinical studies. Mortality reduction in patients with STEMI who are undergoing fibrinolysis is the highest within the first 4 hours, being around 80% after 2 hours and less than 20% after 4 hours of symptom onset. [13] Patients with developed collateral circulation may have more significant benefits beyond the 2-hour timeframe when compared to patients without collateral circulation. The CAPTIM trial compared prehospital fibrinolysis with PCI in patients with STEMI and found that patients who received treatment with pre-hospital fibrinolysis within 2 hours of symptoms onset had better survival when compared to PCI strategy. [14] Based on the STREAM trial, prehospital fibrinolysis provides similar efficacy when compared to PCI in patients with STEMI who presented within 3 hours and were unable to get PCI within 1 hour but with a higher rate of intracranial bleeding. [15] [16] An overview of nine randomized trials published by the Fibrinolytic Therapy Trialist collaborative group suggests that characteristics of patients who derive the least benefit from fibrinolysis are those with a delayed presentation (over 13 to 24 hours of symptom onset), elderly (age greater than 75 years), inferior ST elevations, and uncontrolled hypertension (systolic blood pressure (BP) more than 175 mmHg). [17] Thus, as a general framework, it seems that patients who present with early onset of symptoms but with a significant anticipated delay in transportation to a PCI-capable facility should get fibrinolytic treatment in the absence of absolute contraindications. While on the other hand, patients who present with onset of symptoms longer than 12 hours and absence of hemodynamic instability and evidence of ongoing ischemia of a large amount of myocardium should not receive fibrinolysis due to a likely lack of significant benefit. Other cases require evaluation on a case-by-case basis with careful consideration of risks and benefits. Upon deciding for a fibrinolytic strategy, agents that are fibrin-specific and given as a bolus (such as tenecteplase and reteplase) are preferred to other agents due to demonstrated lower risk of intracerebral bleeding and are more convenient to use. [18] [19]

title: Saphenous Vein Grafts -- Introduction
content: Coronary artery disease (CAD) is a highly prevalent and initial consensus disease afflicting many. Recently, the 2016 Heart Disease and Stroke Statistics update of the American Heart Association (AHA) reported a disease prevalence of 15.5 million persons among those 20 years and older in the USA. [1] The treatment of CAD ranges from medical management and lifestyle modification to invasive coronary revascularization. Coronary revascularization was first performed in 1960 by Dr. Robert H. Goetz who performed a right internal mammary artery (RIMA) anastomosis to the right coronary artery, and in 1967 Dr. René Favaloro first described the use of an autologous reversed greater saphenous vein (GSV) graft as a bypass graft. [2] An easily accessible and reliable conduit with a significant length, the GSV continued to be the conduit of choice until 1986 when data revealed the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) as a superior vessel to revascularize the left anterior descending artery (LAD) territory of the myocardium. [3] The LIMA graft showed a significant increase in graft patency and patient survival as compared to GSV and thus became the initial consensus vessel of choice for CABG.

title: Chronic Ischemic Heart Disease Selection of Treatment Modality -- Evaluation
content: After the clinical history and non-invasive testing are used to define patient risk, the decision to pursue invasive testing can be made. This is done through cardiac catheterization, where a coronary angiogram can define coronary anatomy with the aim of identifying flow-limiting atherosclerotic lesions. However, the indications to perform such a procedure largely depend on whether or not the information obtained is likely to affect management. While a coronary angiogram may occasionally be used to diagnose IHD in a patient with an atypical clinical presentation or indeterminate stress test results, it is often not done for purely diagnostic reasons. The primary benefit for the majority of patients is in providing another layer of risk-stratification that can direct revascularization-eligible patients towards that route, where they may receive primary cutaneous intervention (PCI) or be referred for coronary artery bypass graft (CABG) surgery.

title: Selecting a Treatment Modality in Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Function
content: In the absence of absolute contraindication, anticoagulation is indicated in all patients with UA/NSTEMI once the diagnosis is made. [11] [55] [71] Enoxaparin, UFH, and fondaparinux are listed as class I of recommendation for this purpose. [11] [55] [67] Enoxaparin and fondaparinux should be avoided in patients with severe renal failure. Data from the SYNERGY trial demonstrated the non-inferiority of enoxaparin when compared to UFH in terms of efficacy, with the expense of a modest increase in bleeding risk. [72] [73] On the other hand, a meta-analysis published eight years after the SYNERGY trial reported a superior effect of enoxaparin when compared to UFH in reducing mortality and bleeding outcomes during PCI in patients with ACS. [37] The benefit was observed mostly in patients with STEMI. OASIS-5 study has demonstrated non-inferiority in the efficacy of fondaparinux compared to enoxaparin, with a better bleeding risk profile. [74] An important caveat was an increased risk of catheter-related thrombosis during PCI, which was successfully mitigated by intraprocedural UFH administration. [74] [75] Based on these observations, a reasonable approach would be that in patients undergoing early invasive strategy, where PCI is expected, the anticoagulant of choice should be UFH, while enoxaparin could be an option in the absence of bleeding risk and renal failure. Fondaparinux should be considered in patients with higher bleeding risk in the absence of advanced renal failure but would mandate intraprocedural UFH administration to prevent intra-procedural catheter thrombosis. In patients managed by ischemia-guided strategy and PCI is unlikely to happen, fondaparinux offers optimal safety profile, while observations from ESSENCE and TIMI 11B trial suggest that enoxaparin in this clinical scenario should be favored when compared to UFH. [76] [77] Bivalirudin is recommended as an anticoagulant agent in patients with UA/NSTEMI who have heparin-induced thrombocytopenia. Otherwise, bivalirudin offers a questionable benefit to a very small group of patients. Those patients would have characteristics that favor a strong inclination to add GpIIb/IIIa inhibitors and, at the same time, would have to be at increased risk of bleeding. In that situation, given non-inferiority in ischemic risk and lower incidence of bleeding with bivalirudin monotherapy compared to LMWH or UFH+GpIIb/IIIa, bivalirudin would be a choice. [68] Anticoagulation in the acute phase of UA/NSTEMI treatment is discontinued after revascularization, or in the case when no revascularization is performed, discontinuation is performed after at least 48 hours. [11] [55] [71]

title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a spectrum of disorders that all involve injury to the myocardium. Specific forms include Acute Q‑wave infarction – widespread, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and Acute STEMI due to occlusion of distinct coronary branches such as the left coronary artery, the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the diagonal branch of the LAD, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and the septal branch of the LAD. Additional variants are Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to circumflex branch occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and Acute widespread myocardial infarction. Despite the differing anatomic sites of arterial blockage and electrocardiographic patterns (Q‑wave versus ST‑segment elevation), each of these entities shares the common finding site of the myocardium, underscoring that the primary pathological substrate in all these conditions is myocardial tissue damage. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved helps guide reperfusion strategies and risk stratification, while the universal involvement of the myocardium highlights the need for prompt diagnosis and treatment to preserve cardiac function.

title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2136
content: Acute myocardial infarction can present in many distinct clinical and electrocardiographic forms, yet all of these entities share the common pathological involvement of the myocardium. The condition includes classic presentations such as Acute Q‑wave myocardial infarction and Acute non‑Q‑wave infarction – widespread, as well as various ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (STEMI) that are defined by the specific coronary artery segment that is occluded. Examples of STEMI subtypes are Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the proximal, distal, marginal, septal, or posterior descending branches of the right coronary artery, and the proximal left anterior descending coronary artery. Additional classifications encompass Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery, Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, and Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the septal branch of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery. Non‑ST‑segment elevation presentations are also represented, such as Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Postoperative settings can give rise to Postoperative acute myocardial infarction. Despite the diversity of terminology, anatomical location, and timing, each of these acute coronary syndromes is characterized by injury to the myokard (myocardial tissue). Recognizing that the myocardium is the universal site of injury across all these variants is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2472
content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a broad spectrum of disorders that all share the myocardium as the primary site of injury. The spectrum includes classic Q‑wave infarctions, ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (STEMI) and non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (NSTEMI), as well as non‑Q‑wave and widespread infarctions. These acute events can be further categorized by the specific coronary artery or branch that is occluded, such as the left anterior descending (LAD) artery (including its proximal segment, diagonal branch, and septal branch), the circumflex artery, the right coronary artery (RCA) and its posterior descending branch, and other branches supplying the myocardium. Additional clinical contexts include myocardial infarction that occurs during a procedure or in the postoperative period. Despite the diversity in electrocardiographic patterns, anatomic locations of occlusion, and clinical settings, each of these entities represents an acute injury to the myocardial tissue. Recognizing that all these variants involve the myocardium is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic targeting of the responsible coronary vessel, and optimal patient management.

title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2197
content: Acute infarct is a cardiac pathology that can manifest with a wide range of morphologic patterns. It is associated with acute Q wave infarction – including the widespread form – and with acute Q wave myocardial infarction. Various forms of acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction are also linked to acute infarct, such as the general disorder and specific presentations caused by occlusion of distinct coronary segments: the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the mid portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and right coronary artery occlusion overall. In addition, acute infarct encompasses acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, and acute myocardial infarction that occurs during a medical procedure. Non‑Q wave morphologies are included as well, with both acute non‑Q wave infarction and its widespread variant being associated with acute infarct. Furthermore, acute infarct is linked to acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction and to subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction, reflecting its relevance to both initial and later ischemic events. Together, these associations illustrate that acute infarct can present with diverse electrocardiographic and anatomic features, emphasizing the importance of recognizing its various morphologies for accurate diagnosis and management.

title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
content: Acute myocardial infarction, in its many clinical presentations, is consistently localized to the myocardium (myokard). Whether described as an acute Q‑wave infarction (including widespread forms), an acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction caused by occlusion of the left coronary artery, the right coronary artery, the proximal left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, the septal branch of the anterior descending branch, or any combination of these arterial territories, the pathological finding site remains the myocardial tissue. This also applies to acute non‑Q‑wave infarctions, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (including widespread and subsequent anterior variants), and myocardial infarctions that occur during procedures. Recognizing that all these diverse infarct types share the myocardium as the affected organ underscores the central role of myocardial injury in the diagnosis, monitoring, and treatment of acute coronary syndromes.

title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 839
content: Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures encompass a range of interventional techniques used to treat coronary artery disease. Percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty (ptca) is classified as a specific type of both Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures and interventional cardiac catheterization, reflecting its role as a catheter‑based, minimally invasive revascularization method. The Arabic description “رأْب الوعاء عبر اللمعة من خلال الجلد” corresponds to ptca, indicating the same procedure in another language. Coronary thrombolysis, another modality within Coronary Therapeutic Services and Procedures, is also categorized under interventional cardiac catheterization, highlighting its use of catheter‑delivered pharmacologic agents to dissolve thrombus. While ptca effectively restores vessel patency, patients may experience recurrent angina after percutaneous transluminal coronary angioplasty, underscoring the need for ongoing monitoring and possible adjunctive therapy. Together, these interrelated procedures form the core of contemporary coronary intervention strategies aimed at relieving ischemic symptoms and improving cardiac outcomes.

title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1871
content: Acute infarct serves as a unifying morphological category that encompasses a wide spectrum of specific myocardial injury patterns. Within this category, Acute Q wave myocardial infarction is recognized as a distinct form of acute infarct. A broad range of Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarctions (STEMI) are also classified under acute infarct, each defined by the precise coronary artery segment that is occluded. These include Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to distal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, due to mid left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, due to occlusion of the diagonal branch of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, due to occlusion of the distal portion of the right coronary artery, due to occlusion of the intermediate artery, due to occlusion of the mid portion of the right coronary artery, due to occlusion of the posterior lateral branch of the right coronary artery, due to occlusion of the proximal portion of the right coronary artery, and Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery. In addition, Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery is another specific morphology linked to acute infarct. More extensive presentations such as Acute widespread myocardial infarction and postoperative forms like Postoperative acute myocardial infarction are also captured within the acute infarct morphology. Together, these entities illustrate how acute infarct integrates diverse anatomic and clinical variants of myocardial infarction, aiding clinicians in precise diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted management of patients with acute coronary occlusion.

title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2248
content: Acute infarct is a broad morphological category that includes a wide range of specific acute myocardial infarction presentations. Within this category, Acute Q wave infarction – widespread and Acute Q wave myocardial infarction represent Q‑wave patterns on electrocardiography. Numerous ST‑segment elevation variants are also encompassed, each defined by the coronary artery or branch that is occluded: Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the diagonal branch of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery; due to occlusion of the intermediate artery; due to occlusion of the marginal branch of the right coronary artery; due to occlusion of the posterior lateral branch of the right coronary artery; due to occlusion of the proximal portion of the right coronary artery; due to occlusion of the septal branch of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery; due to proximal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion; due to right coronary artery occlusion; and involving the left anterior descending coronary artery. Additional morphological forms include Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction during a procedural intervention, and Postoperative acute myocardial infarction. All of these entities share the common descriptor of “has_associated_morphology” with Acute infarct, highlighting that they are specific morphological expressions of an acute infarct event, differentiated by electrocardiographic patterns and the precise coronary vessel or procedural context involved.
